rulings.Case Title: INSURANCE COMPANY OF THE WEST VS. DAVID PERALTA JR ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 31, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF INSURANCE COMPANY OF THE WEST'S Motion To Compel Pro Tem Judge Jeff Wohl, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Insurance Company of the West moves for an order compelling defendant David Peralta, Jr., to Answer plaintiff's Form Interrogatories, Set One; Special Interrogatories, Set One; and Request for Production of Documents, Set One; and for an award of sanctions of $810 ($750 in fees and $60 in costs) incurred in bringing the motion. Because the motion is well founded and unopposed, it is GRANTED. This case arises from an automobile accident on January 8, 2016. Plaintiff alleges that Rami Al Fakhoury, an employee of C&J Leasing, Inc. ("C&J"), for which plaintiff is the workers' compensation insurance carrier, drove a car that was hit by a vehicle owned by defendant. Plaintiff further alleges that Mr. Fakhoury was injured as a result of the collision and, because at the time he was acting in the course and scope of his employment with C&J, plaintiff has had to provide workers' compensation benefits for him. Plaintiff sues defendant for reimbursement of the cost of those benefits under Labor Code section 3852. Plaintiff's discovery requests seek information and documents that appear relevant to plaintiff's claim against defendant. Plaintiff served the requests on defendant on September 13, 2017. Defendant has not responded to the requests. Defendant also has not opposed this motion to compel and for sanctions. When a party fails to respond timely to interrogatories and a request for documents, all objections are waived and the Court may grant a motion to compel answers to the discovery requests and production of the requested documents. Cal. Code Civ. Proc. §§ 2030.290(a), (b) (providing that failure to timely respond to interrogatories "waives any right to exercise the option to ... object[] to the interrogatories" and that propounding party may move to compel answers); id., §§ 2031.300(a), (b) (providing same for document requests). In addition, sanctions shall be awarded to compensate the moving party for the expense of bringing the motion to compel, absent a showing that the party against whom sanctions are sought acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. Id., §§ 2030.290(c), 2031.300(c). Here, given that defendant has not opposed the motion, there is no such showing. Moreover, the sanctions sought-$750 in attorneys' fees and $60 in costs-appear to be reasonable. (end of tenative ruling part 1) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: INSURANCE COMPANY OF THE WEST VS. DAVID PERALTA JR ET AL

rulings.Case Title: GEOFFREY MURRY VS. JOHN T. HENDRICKS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION / Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment And Proof Of Service
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 31, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANTS HENDRICKS LAW, P.C. AND JOHN T. HENDRICKS' Motion For Summary Judgment Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication Of Issues. Defendants Hendricks Law P.C. and John T. Hendricks' motion for summary judgment is denied. The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the Labor Code claims, UCL claim, and breach of the Of Counsel contract claim (causes of action 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, and 10). There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether the parties had an employment relationship until Mr. Murry's termination on April 10, 2017. On January 19, 2011, Mr. Murry began working for the Law Offices of John T. Hendricks (a sole proprietorship) under the Of Counsel agreement. The agreement provides that Mr. Murry is an independent contractor, but Mr. Murry presents evidence showing that Mr. Hendricks controlled his work. (Murry Decl., 3-5, 7, 12.) Control is the touchstone of an employment relationship and there is a disputed issue of fact whether Mr. Murry worked as an employee under the Of Counsel agreement. (See Dynamex Operations W. v. Superior Court (2018) 4 Cal.5th 903, 929 [explaining how the degree of control has historically been the most important factor in determining an employment relationship].) Mr. Hendricks mischaracterizes Mr. Murry's deposition testimony at 64:23-65:12. Mr. Murry testified that he exercised discretion with regard to how matters were handled, not that Hendricks did not supervise his work. Thereafter, the parties discussed making Mr. Murry a shareholder in the professional corporation, but the shareholder agreement was never consummated. (Murry Decl., 6.) Murry subsequently continued to work in the same fashion, i.e., as an alleged employee, until his termination. (Murry Decl., 7 and 12.) Defendants' continued (and inconsistent) characterizations of Mr. Murry as a "shareholder," "partner," and "owner" of the law firm are inaccurate. The evidence discloses that Mr. Murry had an opportunity to purchase shares in the professional corporation but the purchase never occurred. (UMF 32.) Further, there is no competent evidence that the parties ever entered into a partnership agreement or formed a "partnership." (UMF 31.) Partnerships and professional corporations are different animals. The Moscone-Knox Professional Corporation Act governs professional corporations. (See Corps. Code §§ 13400-13410.) The Uniform Partnership Act governs partnerships. (See Corps. Code §§ 16100 et seq.) The court denies summary adjudication on the right of publicity claims (causes of action 6 and 7). Defendants cite 74 facts in their statement of undisputed material facts to show that the claims "cannot be established" within the meaning of Code of Civil Procedure, § 437c(p)(2). Of those facts, only facts 71 and 72 appear germane to these claims. Those facts show that defendants purportedly deactivated their prior website that referenced Mr. Murry. (Hendricks Decl., 17.) Mr. Murry's evidence shows that information regarding him continued to be displayed after he ceased working with defendants, however, and there is a triable issue of material fact regarding damage, if any. (Cohorn Decl., 5-6; Exs. H, I.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Murry is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JAMEL BOUZIDI ET AL VS. RAOUF BOUZIDI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Plaintiffs Notice Of And Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 31, 2019, Line 8, PLAINTIFFS JAMEL BOUZIDI, SOFIENE BOUZIDI's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Plaintiffs Jamel Bouzidi and Sofiene Bouzidi's motion for summary judgment is granted. Plaintiffs establish that they are entitled to $177,321.88 in damages based upon defendant's breach of the settlement agreement. Plaintiffs establish a prima facie case for breach of contract and the court overrules defendant's evidentiary objections. Plaintiffs demonstrate personal knowledge regarding the matters in their declarations and the IRS documents are admissible as official acts. (Evid. Code, §§ 452(c) and 1280.) The parties entered into a binding settlement agreement and defendant, who was represented by counsel, fails to show that the settlement was the product of economic duress. (Raouf Bouzidi Dec., 23; CrossTalk Productions, Inc. v. Jacobson (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 631, 644 ["If a reasonable alternative was available, and there hence was no compelling necessity to submit to the coercive demands, economic duress cannot be established."].) Defendant cannot manufacture a triable issue of fact by contradicting his sworn July 21, 2016 declaration, in which he stated, "I have not been induced or threatened in any manner in providing this declaration and do so knowingly, intelligently, freely, and voluntarily." (See D'Amico v. Board of Medical Examiners (1974) 11 Cal.3d 1, 21.) Plaintiffs have performed all material terms of the settlement agreement. (UMF 7-10.) Paragraph 3.2 of the settlement agreement provides that defendant is liable for the plaintiffs' taxes. Defendant has breached the settlement agreement by failing to pay the taxes. (UMF 12.) As the court noted when it overruled defendant's prior demurrer, the contract is not illegal. This is especially true because defendant previously admitted that: "I alone am solely liable and responsible for any and all taxes penalties, fines, interest, costs, fees and attorney's fees regarding the operation of Chez Fayala, LLC." (Mau Dec., Ex. C (Raouf Bouzidi Decl., 5; Evid. Code, §§ 1123 and 1220.) There is no violation of Civil Code, § 1668 because there is no evidence that plaintiffs acted illegally and defendant admits that he was responsible for the taxes. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The court will execute plaintiff's form of order. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CENTER FOR ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH A NON-PROFIT VS. WHOLESOME SWEETENERS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Court Approval And Entry Of Consent Judgments As To Allied Old English, Inc. And Wholesome Sweeteners, Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 31, 2019, Line 10, PLAINTIFF CENTER FOR ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH A NON-PROFIT's Motion For Court Approval And Entry Of Consent Judgments As To Allied Old English, Inc. And Wholesome Sweeteners, Inc. Plaintiff Center for Environmental Health's motion for court approval and entry of consent judgments as to Allied Old English, Inc. and Wholesome Sweeteners, Inc. is granted. No opposition filed and the settlement complies with the requirements for Proposition 65 settlements under Health & Safety Code section 25249.7. The Court finds that the warning that is required by the settlement complies with Health & Safety Code sections 25249.5-25249.14; the award of attorney's fees is reasonable under California law; and the penalty amount is reasonable based on the criteria set forth in Health & Safety Code section 25249.7(b)(2).
